sourceMovie.title: What Women Want
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: What Women Want (2000) stuck because it commits to its comedy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Nancy Meyers's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The comedy and romance blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Pretty Woman
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If What Women Want's comedy stayed with you, Pretty Woman (1990) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The romance through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Lost in Translation
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where What Women Want excels in comedy, Lost in Translation (2003) doubles down on romance to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Breakfast at Tiffany's
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Breakfast at Tiffany's (1961) is the natural next step after What Women Want: same comedy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and romance elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: What Men Want
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of What Women Want who respond to its comedy will find What Men Want (2019) immediately familiar. Both films share a romance sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: To Be or Not to Be
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason What Women Want fans keep landing on To Be or Not to Be (1942) — the comedy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: The Diary of a Teenage Girl
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Diary of a Teenage Girl draws from the same comedy well as What Women Want, channeling romance in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2015 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Love Don't Co$t a Thing
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Love Don't Co$t a Thing (2003) captures the comedy appeal of What Women Want without simply imitating it. The romance streak running through both films is what puts Love Don't Co$t a Thing at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Just One of the Guys
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The comedy appeal of What Women Want — that funny edge, the romance — translates cleanly into Just One of the Guys (1985). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: ¿Quieres ser mi hijo?
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind ¿Quieres ser mi hijo? (2023) clearly understood what makes comedy work — the same quality that keeps What Women Want rewatchable. Expect the same funny rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Wrong Paris
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Wrong Paris (2025) is the kind of film What Women Want fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The comedy overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after What Women Want?
faq.answer: Try Pretty Woman, Lost in Translation, Breakfast at Tiffany's—each shares DNA with What Women Want while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is What Women Want?
faq.answer: What Women Want (2000) is best described as Comedy, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is What Women Want worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.4/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
